Introduction to modern web development

Two parts

Basics
  • HTML, CSS, JavaScript
  • Event driven programming
  • Browser APIs
Advanced
  • Servers and server-side code
  • Asynchronous JavaScript
  • Bundlers, Frameworks & Tools
  • TypeScript

HTML

Hyper Text Markup Language

HTML is a text format that gets rendered by the browser. Each browser has its own default styling.
becomes

HTML

Hyper Text Markup Language

HTML forms a tree structure with each tag a node

CSS

Cascading Style Sheets

You style HTML elements using CSS

CSS

Cascading Style Sheets

Common CSS selectors

CSS
HTML

CSS

Cascading Style Sheets

Further CSS resources/tips

  • Mozilla Developer Network CSS tutorial for common rules.
  • Flexbox Froggy For positioning/layout
  • The most specific CSS selector applies
  • New CSS rules overwrite old ones

Our website so far

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

Key concepts:

  • High-level, dynamically typed, single-threaded.
  • Garbage collected (no manual memory management).
  • Browsers have a JavaScript interpreter to run JS client-side.
  • NodeJS is an interpreter to run JS server-side. More on this later.
  • Has nothing to do with Java.

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

Basic syntax

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

Basic types

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

for interactivity

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

Object-oriented

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

Remember our HTML tree? This is accessible as an Object in JavaScript, called the Document Object Model (DOM).

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

Event-driven programming

Our website so far

Let's reorganize the code

Place CSS in .css files, JS in .js files, and link it in the HTML file.

JavaScript (JS)

for interactivity

Browser APIs

The browser exposes loads of features via a global Object called window
N.B. You may omit window if not ambiguous.
To understand fetch, we will need to know how
asynchronous programming works.

Part one done

Basics
  • HTML, CSS, JavaScript
  • Event driven programming
  • Browser APIs
Advanced
  • Servers and server-side code
  • Asynchronous JavaScript
  • Bundlers, Frameworks & Tools
  • TypeScript

Server-side code

Static v.s. Dynamic website
  • Static: Same for everyone who visits it.
    Blogs, restaurant menus, company homepage. A catalogue.
  • Dynamic: Personalized experience. Facebook, Twitter, Brightspace. Websites with accounts.

Server-side code

Static website
Client (browser)
HTTP GET
/index.html
HTTP 200
index.html
HTTP Request
Server

Server-side code

Dynamic website (1)
Client (browser)
HTTP GET
/account.html
HTTP 200
account.html
HTTP Request
Server

Server-side code

Dynamic website (2)
Client (browser)
HTTP GET
/account.html
HTTP 200
account.html
HTTP Request
Server
account.html

Logged in as

John Doe
Balance: €100
Client now has empty shell and will fetch data from the server via JS

Asynchronous JS

Async/await

Problem: JavaScript is single-threaded.

When we make a long I/O call (like fetch), are we going to block the thread until it is done?
An HTTP request can take any amount of time. The UI could be frozen for a full 2 seconds or maybe even 20!

Asynchronous JS

Async/await

Problem: JavaScript is single-threaded.
Solution: Don't block, and keep code running while the I/O call is processing.
We can register some code to run once the I/O call has resolved.

Asynchronous JS

Async/await

We will register some code to run once the I/O call has resolved.
How?
In the past: Event-listeners.
But this gets very cumbersome when chained

Asynchronous JS

Async/await

We will register some code to run once the I/O call has resolved.
How?
Nowadays: Promises
Promise is a built-in type that represents a value that will be available in the future.
fetch is an asynchronous function, meaning its return type is Promise

Asynchronous JS

Async/await

Promise is a built-in type that represents a value that will be available in the future.
How do you get the value of a Promise?
1. Use then, and register a function.

Asynchronous JS

Async/await

Promise is a built-in type that represents a value that will be available in the future.
How do you get the value of a Promise?
2. Use await
This is only available in async functions!

Asynchronous JS

Async/await

await is only available in async functions!

An async function automatically returns a Promise

Instead of .catch, use try / catch to handle errors.

Asynchronous JS

Async/await

Common mistake with async functions
This happens:

Promise values are only available after await or in a then() callback.

Frameworks and tools

Make things easier(?)

Over the years, several issues became apparent with "normal" frontend development:

  • Imperative DOM manipulation in JavaScript is a pain.
    * You can view the DOM as a global variable that can be mutated from anywhere in the code.
  • App is split in HTML/CSS/JS but this isn't how humans think about a UI
  • Lots of boilerplate and security pitfalls (XSS)
  • Hard to avoid duplicate code

Component frameworks have therefore become very popular.

Declarative vs Imperative

Imperative
Declarative:

Frameworks and tools

Make things easier(?)

Component frameworks have therefore become very popular.

  • Angular (2010) by Google
  • React (2013) by Facebook
  • Vue (2014) by Evan You.
    Light-weight, minimal Angular alternative
  • Svelte (2018) by Rich Harris.
    Because all the above are "lacking/non-performant"

Each has its own, non-standard syntax to enhance HTML/CSS/JS.

Frameworks and tools

Make things easier(?)

Our app is currently split into files like this:

HTML
CSS
JS
With a component framework, this becomes:
Main button
Login Form
Account overview
App

Frameworks and tools

Make things easier(?)

They all have the same common ideas:

1. Developers can simply declare what their UI looks like.
No "ugly" DOM manipulation.

2. Your UI consists of reusable components.
Components are bundles of HTML/CSS/JS.

Frameworks and tools

Make things easier(?)

Example of a Vue-component for a counter

Frameworks and tools

Make things easier(?)

And now use the component anywhere:

Frameworks and tools

Make things easier(?)

Component frameworks make it easy to import and use components written by others!

This can save you a lot of work.
E.g., Use Google's Material UI library and you will only have to position pre-made components on a page.

Frameworks and tools

Make things easier(?)

More tools for...

HTML
Pug - Less verbose HTML syntax
Markdown - More limited version of HTML
(e.g. for blog posts, forum comments)
CSS
TailwindCSS - Adds many small utility classes,
e.g. text-lg, bg-white
Bootstrap - Very popular CSS framework that adds tons of useful basic components
JavaScript
TypeScript - Strongly typed version of JavaScript that compiles down to JS.
Flow - Static type checker for JavaScript.

Frameworks and tools

TypeScript

If you are used to statically typed languages (C, C++, Java, C#, Rust) TypeScript is very much recommended
